Gemara's Opening Question: Two Possible Reasons for Chachamim's Position

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the Chachamim's Rationale: Belief in the Individual vs. Reinterpretation of Words

micro_segments.title: First Approach: A Person is Believed About Themselves More Than a Hundred Witnesses
Rashi and Tosafot Qualify: Special Belief Because People Don't Lie About Atonement
Alternative Understanding: Between You and God, Not a Court Matter
Second Approach: Migo—Reinterpreting Words to Escape Consequence
Tosafot's Refinement: Not Classic Migo, But Reframing the Statement

macro_segments.title: Testing the Two Approaches: The Nafka Mina (Practical Difference) with Tumah Cases

micro_segments.title: Setup: If Believed Categorically, Old and New Tumah Would Be the Same
Old Tumah Can Be Reinterpreted as Not Remaining Tamei
New Tumah Cannot Be Reinterpreted: No Exit Path Even Through Mikvah

macro_segments.title: Braita Evidence and Rav Ami's Reconciliation of Seemingly Contradictory Statements

micro_segments.title: Braita Case: One Witness Says You Became Tamei; Braita Seems to Support Categorical Belief

macro_segments.title: Ravina's Technical Point: Non-Responsive Answers and the Limits of Reinterpretation

micro_segments.title: Setup: Witnesses Emphasize Eating Kodashim While Tamei, Not Just Becoming Tamei
The Problem: Reinterpreting 'I Didn't Become Tamei' Requires a Non-Responsive Answer
Conclusion: Sometimes We Cannot Reinterpret Because It Would Contradict the Original Statement

macro_segments.title: Summary of Three Positions: Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Yehuda, and the Middle Position

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir: Go by the Witnesses (with Limited Exceptions)
Rabbi Yehuda: It's Between You and God, Ignore Witnesses
Middle Position: Reinterpret Your Words When Possible, Go by Witnesses When Not

macro_segments.title: Halacha Ruling and the Principle: Between You and Yourself, Only in Private

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman: We Rule Like Rabbi Yehuda—You're Believed More Than Witnesses
Rav Yosef's Limitation: Only When It's Between You and Yourself, Not Visible to Others
Rashi's Explanation: Don't Eat Terumah Visibly in Front of Witnesses Who Believe Them
The Broader Principle: Private Sin Between You and God; Public Acts Affect Others

macro_segments.title: Practical Cases Where Even Rabbi Meir Agrees to Set Aside the Witnesses

micro_segments.title: Case of Shifcha Charufa: Reinterpret 'I Didn't' as 'I Didn't Complete the Act'
Case of Nazir Tamei: Could Claim the Nezirul Was Voided
Case of Unknown Testimony: Reinterpret as Lacking Intent to Serve as Witness

micro_segments.title: Same Sin Repeated (Chelev and Chelev): One Forgetfulness = One Korban

Abaye's Answer: Forgetfulness Divides Sins; One Forgetfulness = One Act

macro_segments.title: Different Sins in One Forgetfulness: Multiple Korbanot Required

Same Genre from Different Dishes: Reish Lakish and the Question of Rabbi Yehoshua
Rabbi Yehoshua's Position: Different Dishes Separate—Two Full Zeisim = Two Korbanos
Does Yehoshua Also Apply This to Half-Zeisim (Leniency) or Only to Multiple Zeisim (Stringency)?
Two Interpretations of the Mishna Regarding Different Dishes and Combining
Ravina's Question: What If Awareness Occurs in the Middle of Eating?
